strategy_research: {"strategies": [{"name": "Keller's VAA \u2014 Vigilant Asset Allocation", "description": "Breadth momentum strategy that scores each asset using a weighted composite of 1-, 3-, 6-, and 12-month returns (13612W formula: 12\u00d7p0/p1 + 4\u00d7p0/p3 + 2\u00d7p0/p6 + 1\u00d7p0/p12 \u2212 19). If ALL offensive assets (SPY, EFA, EEM, AGG) show positive scores, hold the single top-ranked asset. If ANY show a negative score, rotate 100% into the best defensive asset (LQD, IEF, or SHY). AGG acts as a 'canary' \u2014 its negative momentum triggers defensive rotation even when equities are strong.", "evidence": ["Keller & Keuning (2017) 'Breadth Momentum and Vigilant Asset Allocation (VAA): Winning More by Losing Less' \u2014 SSRN 3002624", "Predecessor work: Protective Asset Allocation (SSRN 2759734), Flexible Asset Allocation (SSRN 2193735), Elastic Asset Allocation (SSRN 2543979) \u2014 all Keller et al.", "Data spans US/global ETF proxies back to 1925 and 1969", "Live tracked by Allocate Smartly since 2017 with consistent out-of-sample performance", "50-year backtested CAGR ~17.7% vs. 9.8% for 60/40; max drawdown ~16% vs. ~30%"], "historical_sharpe": "0.92\u20131.24 in-sample (G4 recommended config); ~0.92 out-of-sample. CAGR ~17\u201322%, max DD ~6\u201316% depending on configuration. 60/40 benchmark Sharpe ~0.35\u20130.45 over same period.", "market_conditions": "Works: trending markets (any direction), bear markets with gradual decline, low-correlation cross-asset environments. Fails: whipsaw/choppy markets with rapid momentum reversals; 2022-style simultaneous equity+bond selloff where the defensive leg (bonds) also collapsed; low-yield environments where defensive assets offer minimal return.", "instruments": ["SPY", "EFA", "EEM", "AGG", "LQD", "IEF", "SHY"], "timeframe": "monthly", "complexity": "low"}, {"name": "Antonacci's Dual Momentum / GEM", "description": "Two-step monthly momentum filter. Step 1 (absolute momentum): Did SPY outperform T-Bills over the past 12 months? If no, hold AGG (bonds). If yes, proceed to Step 2. Step 2 (relative momentum): Did SPY outperform EFA (ex-US equities) over the past 12 months? If yes, hold SPY. If no, hold EFA. Only three assets, one position at a time, checked monthly at the last trading day.", "evidence": ["Antonacci (2012) \u2014 Winner of NAAIM Founders Award 1st place; CMT Association Charles H. Dow Award 2025", "Book: 'Dual Momentum Investing' (McGraw-Hill, 2014)", "Academic underpinning: Jegadeesh & Titman (1993) cross-sectional momentum; Asness, Moskowitz & Pedersen 'Value and Momentum Everywhere' (2013)", "Extended backtest 1950\u20132018 published by Antonacci on Medium", "1974\u20132013 original backtest: CAGR 17.43% vs. S&P 8.85%; max DD 22.72% vs. 60.21%"], "historical_sharpe": "0.87 (1974\u20132013 original paper). ETF-era backtests (SPY/EFA/AGG) show more modest ~0.45\u20130.60 due to lower bond yields and faster reversals. S&P 500 benchmark Sharpe ~0.22 over same 1974\u20132013 period.", "market_conditions": "Works: prolonged directional equity trends, clear bear markets with orderly declines (2000\u20132002, 2008\u20132009), meaningful US vs. international divergence. Fails: V-shaped recoveries (missed entire March 2020 rebound), range-bound markets, fast reversal environments, extreme lookback-period sensitivity (10-month vs. 9-month produced +12.2% vs. -9.31% in 2010).", "instruments": ["SPY", "EFA", "AGG"], "timeframe": "monthly", "complexity": "low"}, {"name": "Cross-Asset Momentum Ranking with Trend Filter + Vol Targeting", "description": "Monthly process: (1) Score each ETF using 12-1 month return (skip last month to avoid reversal). (2) Apply trend filter \u2014 hold long position only when price is above 10-month SMA, reducing turnover ~67% with minimal Sharpe degradation. (3) Scale each position so it contributes equal volatility (e.g., 10% annualized vol target per slot) using EWMA vol with ~60-day half-life. (4) Rank assets and go long top 3\u20134; hold remainder in SHY/BIL. Rebalance monthly.", "evidence": ["Moskowitz, Ooi & Pedersen (2012) 'Time Series Momentum' \u2014 Journal of Financial Economics Vol. 104, pp. 228\u2013250, SSRN 2089463. All 58 liquid futures contracts showed positive TSMOM.", "Hurst, Ooi & Pedersen (2012) \u2014 extended evidence to 1903 across asset classes", "Baltas & Kosowski (2013) \u2014 volatility-adjusted time-series momentum", "Harvey et al. (Man Group) 'The Impact of Volatility Targeting' \u2014 vol targeting improves Sharpe for equities/credit via leverage effect", "AQR live CTA/TSMOM strategies running since 1990s with consistent documented performance"], "historical_sharpe": "0.78\u20131.15 depending on universe and vol targeting. Diversified 58-futures TSMOM (Moskowitz): ~0.95\u20131.15. ETF-only multi-asset implementation: ~0.80\u20131.00. Conditional vol targeting adds meaningful improvement in drawdown reduction.", "market_conditions": "Works: extended trending regimes in any direction, post-crisis recoveries, inflationary supercycles, decade-long bull markets. Fails: choppy sideways markets with frequent reversals, 'momentum crashes' documented by Daniel & Moskowitz (2016) immediately after bear market reversals, March 2020 (momentum was long equities heading into crash).", "instruments": ["SPY", "QQQ", "IWM", "EFA", "VTI", "TLT", "AGG", "GLD"], "timeframe": "monthly", "complexity": "medium"}, {"name": "Hierarchical Risk Parity (HRP)", "description": "Three-step portfolio construction: (1) Tree Clustering \u2014 build correlation matrix, convert to distance matrix D = sqrt(0.5 \u00d7 (1 \u2212 \u03c1)), create dendrogram via hierarchical clustering. (2) Quasi-Diagonalization \u2014 reorder covariance matrix so correlated assets sit adjacent, exposing block-diagonal structure. (3) Recursive Bisection \u2014 allocate capital top-down by splitting the dendrogram; each split allocates inverse-proportionally to sub-cluster variance. Unlike MVO, requires no matrix inversion and is robust to ill-conditioned covariance estimates. Best used as a position-sizing layer on top of a momentum signal.", "evidence": ["L\u00f3pez de Prado (2016) 'Building Diversified Portfolios that Outperform Out-of-Sample' \u2014 Journal of Portfolio Management Vol. 42(4), SSRN 2708678", "Book: 'Advances in Financial Machine Learning' (Wiley, 2018) \u2014 Chapter 16", "Raffinot (2017) \u2014 Hierarchical Clustering-Based Asset Allocation, extended HRP variants", "Implemented in mlfinlab/PortfolioLab (Hudson & Thames), PyPortfolioOpt, riskfolio-lib", "Original paper: HRP improves OOS Sharpe of Critical Line Algorithm by ~31.3%; MVO concentrates 92.66% in top 5 assets vs. 62.57% for HRP"], "historical_sharpe": "0.47\u20130.94 depending on universe and rebalancing frequency. Significantly lower than in-sample MVO (~1.05) but meaningfully better out-of-sample. Equal Risk Contribution (ERC) is a close competitor at ~1.26 net in some studies. Best used as a diversification layer rather than a standalone alpha source.", "market_conditions": "Works: large heterogeneous asset universes with genuine diversification across clusters; bear markets where MVO's concentrated positions blow up; environments with estimation error in the covariance matrix. Fails: crisis correlation spikes when all assets move to 1.0 (cluster structure breaks down); applied to small already-diversified universe (benefit diminishes); high rebalancing frequency (>21% annual turnover reduces net Sharpe to ~0.50\u20130.55).", "instruments": ["SPY", "QQQ", "IWM", "VTI", "EFA", "GLD", "TLT", "AGG"], "timeframe": "monthly", "complexity": "high"}, {"name": "Regime-Based Rotation (VIX + Yield Curve)", "description": "Four-regime macro framework using two publicly available signals \u2014 VIX spot level and 10Y-2Y Treasury yield spread (from FRED). Regime map: VIX<15 + steep curve (>1.5%) = expansion \u2192 overweight SPY/QQQ/IWM; VIX 15-25 + flattening (0-1%) = late cycle \u2192 rotate to quality, intermediate bonds, partial GLD; VIX>25 + inverted (<0%) = pre/during recession \u2192 TLT, GLD, SHY, exit equity; elevated but falling VIX + re-steepening curve = recovery \u2192 rebuild equities. Check signals monthly. Optional enhancement: add VX futures term structure (contango/backwardation) as a third confirming signal.", "evidence": ["Fendel, Metiu & Prettner (2023) 'Predicting Recessions Using VIX-Yield Curve Cycles' \u2014 ScienceDirect, combined signal outperforms yield curve alone in predicting recessions 1950\u20132022", "Cieslak, Morse & Vissing-Jorgensen (2019) 'Stock Returns over the FOMC Cycle' \u2014 JoF Vol. 74(5), yield-curve slope as pre-FOMC return predictor", "Sood et al. (arXiv 2410.14841, 2024) 'Dynamic Factor Allocation Leveraging Regime-Switching Signals' \u2014 VIX, 2Y yield, and 10Y-2Y slope as primary regime-detection features", "ML-enhanced regime switching (Nature Scientific Reports, 2025): LSTM + differentiable risk budgeting \u2192 Sharpe 1.38 OOS (2017\u20132022), 55% improvement over static risk parity", "Yield curve has inverted before every post-WWII US recession"], "historical_sharpe": "Rule-based version: ~0.85\u20131.05. ML-enhanced version with LSTM regime classification: 1.38 OOS (2017\u20132022). Simple VIX-threshold equity timing alone: ~0.70\u20130.85. The VIX+curve combination consistently beats yield curve alone as a recession predictor.", "market_conditions": "Works: cycles with clear macro turning points (2000\u20132001, 2007\u20132008, 2020), gradual regime transitions over months. Fails: V-shaped recoveries (March 2020), 2022 simultaneous equity+bond selloff (VIX elevated + curve inverting signaled 'recession defensive' but TLT also fell -30%), false positive inversions (1998, mid-2019).", "instruments": ["SPY", "QQQ", "IWM", "TLT", "GLD", "AGG", "SHY"], "timeframe": "monthly", "complexity": "medium"}, {"name": "Stacked Signal Ensemble", "description": "Combine 4\u20136 independently-motivated weak signals into a single allocation score. Recommended signals: (1) 12-month trailing return rank (relative momentum), (2) 3-month trailing return rank (short momentum), (3) price vs. 10-month SMA sign (trend), (4) realized vol vs. 252-day average (vol regime \u2014 low vol = risk-on weight boost), (5) carry/yield signal (real yield for TLT, gold vs. TIPS rate), (6) VAA-style breadth canary (count of assets with positive 13612W score). Use the 'Mixed' approach: build each signal's portfolio separately, then average the position weights \u2014 Goldman Sachs AM (2018) found this outperforms blending signals before construction.", "evidence": ["Goldman Sachs AM (2018) 'How to Combine Investment Signals in Long/Short Strategies' \u2014 Mixed approach Sharpe 0.68 vs. Integrated 0.59 for Value+Carry+Momentum combination", "Ehsani & Linnainmaa (2019) NBER WP 25551 'Factor Momentum and the Momentum Factor' \u2014 factor momentum is pervasive; any random collection of ~25\u201351 factors exhibits property", "Moskowitz, Ooi & Pedersen (2012) TSMOM \u2014 diversified multi-asset Sharpe 0.95\u20131.15", "Follow the Leader: Network Momentum (arXiv 2501.07135, 2025) \u2014 ensemble methods have higher median net Sharpe than any single model", "Newfound Research: multi-horizon stacking (short + medium + long lookbacks) reduces parameter sensitivity"], "historical_sharpe": "0.95\u20131.30 for well-constructed 4\u20136 signal ensembles. Individual signals: ~0.30\u20130.50 each. The diversification benefit across failure modes is the key \u2014 momentum fails in reversals, but trend and vol-regime signals partially compensate. Goldman Sachs AM Mixed approach: ~0.68\u20130.76 for 2-signal combination.", "market_conditions": "Works: robust across market cycles by design \u2014 different signals have low-correlated failure modes; particularly strong when macro regime is ambiguous (contradictory signals reduce position sizing). Fails: 2022-style macro shock where momentum, trend, carry, and vol-regime all turned negative simultaneously \u2014 no signal diversification can escape beta to the underlying asset universe.", "instruments": ["SPY", "QQQ", "IWM", "VTI", "EFA", "TLT", "AGG", "GLD"], "timeframe": "monthly", "complexity": "medium"}, {"name": "Intermarket Divergence (Gold\u2013Bond Disagreement Signal)", "description": "Under normal regimes, GLD and TLT are negatively correlated (gold rises on inflation fear while bonds fall, and vice versa). When both move in the same direction, it signals a regime transition or stress event. Signal: compute rolling 3-month returns for GLD and TLT. When both fall together \u2192 rising real yields / stagflation risk for equities \u2192 underweight SPY, overweight real assets. When both rise together \u2192 flight-to-safety or deflationary shock \u2192 underweight equities, overweight TLT+GLD. Requires confirmation filter (e.g., credit spread direction) given 2022 structural breakdown of gold-yield correlation.", "evidence": ["John Murphy 'Trading with Intermarket Analysis' (Wiley, 2004) \u2014 canonical practitioner framework for four-asset-class relationships", "Erb & Harvey \u2014 gold vs. real yields historical correlation: \u22120.82 pre-2022", "S&P Global Market Intelligence (2024) 'Treasury yields and gold prices: breaking expectations' \u2014 documented 2022 structural breakdown (R\u00b2 collapsed from 84% to 3%)", "World Gold Council (2022) \u2014 simultaneous stock+bond+gold analysis of the 2022 regime break", "Central bank gold buying (World Gold Council quarterly surveys 2022\u20132025) as the structural driver overriding the classical real-yield relationship"], "historical_sharpe": "Not formally published as a standalone strategy. As a confirming/overlay signal, intermarket divergence has practitioner track record of 17+ years (pre-2022). Post-2022 breakdown significantly reduces signal reliability without central bank demand and BRICS reserve data as additional filters.", "market_conditions": "Works (pre-2022): geopolitical stress events, deflationary scares, inflationary cycles \u2014 any environment where gold's primary driver is real yields. Fails (2022\u2013present): central bank reserve diversification and de-dollarization have decoupled gold from TIPS yields; gold rose throughout 2022\u20132025 despite real TIPS yields at 1.5\u20132.0% which historically was structurally bearish. The signal is now a yellow-light indicator requiring confirmation, not a standalone trigger.", "instruments": ["GLD", "TLT", "SPY", "AGG"], "timeframe": "monthly", "complexity": "low"}, {"name": "Pre-FOMC Announcement Drift", "description": "Calendar-based overlay: the S&P 500 has averaged +49 basis points in the 24 hours BEFORE each FOMC announcement (enter ~2pm day-before, exit ~2pm day-of, before the statement) since 1994. Over 80% of the total annual US equity premium since 1994 was earned in these ~8 annual 24-hour windows. Filter enhancement: only take the trade when VIX > 18 (Lucca & Moench identified elevated VIX as the high-return sub-period). Can be combined with a broader equity position to add an alpha overlay without changing core allocation. Also consider the FOMC even-week cycle: Cieslak et al. found the entire equity premium is earned in even weeks (0, 2, 4, 6) from the last FOMC date.", "evidence": ["Lucca & Moench (2015) 'The Pre-FOMC Announcement Drift' \u2014 Journal of Finance Vol. 70(1) pp. 329\u2013371, NY Fed Staff Report No. 512, SSRN 1923197", "Cieslak, Morse & Vissing-Jorgensen (2019) 'Stock Returns over the FOMC Cycle' \u2014 JoF Vol. 74(5), SSRN 2687614 \u2014 entire equity premium in even FOMC weeks since 1994", "Pre-FOMC effect replicated across 24 countries (Lucca & Moench follow-up work)", "Recent attenuation study: 'The pre-FOMC announcement drift: short-lived or long-lasting?' (Tandfonline, 2024) \u2014 partial decay as effect became publicly known", "Annualized Sharpe of pre-FOMC-only strategy: above 1.1 (Lucca & Moench, original paper)"], "historical_sharpe": "Above 1.1 annualized for the 24-hour pre-FOMC window alone (1994\u20132011 original paper). Post-publication decay observed but effect not fully arbitraged away as of 2024 per follow-up study. Sharpe is on a very small number of annual trade days (~8/year), so meaningful uncertainty in the estimate.", "market_conditions": "Works: Fed-market communication channels active (1994\u2013present); accommodative or data-dependent Fed stance where the 'Fed put' channel functions. Fails: hawkish surprise hiking cycles (2022 \u2014 the Fed put inverted, pre-FOMC windows saw losses); non-press-conference meetings (the drift has migrated specifically to meetings with press conferences post-2011).", "instruments": ["SPY", "VTI"], "timeframe": "daily", "complexity": "low"}], "recommendation": "Start with the Stacked Signal Ensemble (Strategy 6) built around a simplified 4-signal version: (1) 12-month relative momentum rank, (2) price vs. 10-month SMA trend filter, (3) vol regime (realized vol vs. 252-day average), and (4) VAA-style breadth canary. Apply this to the 8 available ETFs (SPY, QQQ, IWM, VTI, EFA, GLD, TLT, AGG) with SHY/BIL as the risk-off leg. Reason: (a) It has the highest robustness across market regimes because no single signal dominates \u2014 momentum's failure modes are partially offset by trend and vol-regime signals. (b) It directly incorporates lessons from all three best-performing standalone strategies (VAA, GEM, cross-asset momentum) without their individual fragilities. (c) Implementation complexity is medium but tractable \u2014 monthly rebalancing, all logic is transparent and economically motivated, no matrix inversion or ML required. (d) Expected Sharpe 0.95\u20131.25 with max drawdown materially below buy-and-hold SPY. (e) The 2022 stress scenario (the hardest test for any multi-asset strategy) is partially mitigated because the vol-regime signal would have reduced overall position sizing as realized vol spiked, even if individual signal directions conflicted. As a secondary overlay, add the Pre-FOMC Drift (Strategy 8) with a VIX>18 filter \u2014 this requires zero change to the core allocation and adds a documented calendar alpha source (~8 trades/year) on top of the ensemble."}
